title: Coding Interview Patterns
tagline: Every algorithm gets its own animation — see the pattern move, then code it yourself.
description: The visual way to master coding interviews. Each of 20 pattern chapters pairs a step-through animation you control with a hands-on code lab, covering arrays, trees, graphs, dynamic programming, divide and conquer, quickselect, string matching, Union Find, range trees, minimum spanning trees, shortest paths, and network flow.

exercises.title: Pattern: Two Pointers
exercises.content:
# Two Pointers

Two indexes walking through a **sorted** array (or a string) from both ends, converging toward each other. Instead of testing all O(n²) pairs, each comparison eliminates one pointer position — a single O(n) pass.

### How to recognize it
- Input is sorted (or sortable) and you need a **pair / triplet** with some property
- "Find two numbers that sum to…", "is it a palindrome?", "container with most water"
- Brute force would compare every pair

### The template
```javascript
let l = 0, r = nums.length - 1;
while (l < r) {
  const sum = nums[l] + nums[r];
  if (sum === target) return [l, r];
  if (sum < target) l++;   // need a bigger value
  else r--;                // need a smaller value
}
```

### Why moving the pointer is safe
When `sum < target`, no pair using `nums[l]` can work — `nums[r]` is already the biggest partner available. So skipping `l` forward throws away nothing.

**Complexity:** O(n) time, O(1) space.

**Classic problems:** Two Sum II · 3Sum · Container With Most Water · Valid Palindrome · Trapping Rain Water

exercises.title: Animation: converging pointers
exercises.content:
# Watch the pointers converge

Find a pair summing to **15** in `[1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 11, 13]`.

At every step, ask yourself **which pointer moves and why** — that reasoning is exactly what you'll say out loud in a real interview. Step through until the pair lights up orange.

exercises.title: Code lab: pairSum
exercises.content:
# Code lab: pair with target sum

Implement `pairSum(nums, target)` for a **sorted** array. Return the two **indexes** `[i, j]` of the pair that sums to `target`, or `null` if no pair exists.

Use converging pointers — no nested loops!

### Examples
- `pairSum([1,3,4,6,8,11,13], 15)` → `[2, 5]` (4 + 11)
- `pairSum([2,7,11,15], 9)` → `[0, 1]`
- `pairSum([1,2,3], 7)` → `null`
exercises.hint: Move exactly one pointer per iteration. sum < target means the LEFT value is too small to ever work with any remaining partner.

exercises.title: Pattern: Sliding Window
exercises.content:
# Sliding Window

A window `[left, right]` slides across an array or string. The insight: when the window moves one step, **only one element enters and one leaves** — so you update the answer incrementally instead of recomputing the whole window.

### How to recognize it
- "Longest / shortest / maximum **substring or subarray** that …"
- A **contiguous** range is involved
- Brute force recomputes overlapping work: O(n·k) or O(n²)

### Two flavors
**Fixed size k** (like the animation):
```javascript
let sum = 0, best = -Infinity;
for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
  sum += nums[i];              // element enters
  if (i >= k) sum -= nums[i - k]; // element leaves
  if (i >= k - 1) best = Math.max(best, sum);
}
```
**Variable size** — grow `right` every step, shrink `left` only while the window is invalid.

**Complexity:** O(n) — each element enters once and leaves once.

**Classic problems:** Maximum Average Subarray · Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters · Minimum Window Substring · Sliding Window Maximum

exercises.title: Animation: the window slides
exercises.content:
# Watch the window slide

Track the **max of every window of size 3** across `[1, 3, -1, -3, 5, 3, 6, 7]`.

As you step, notice that only the entering and leaving elements matter — the middle of the window never gets re-examined. Slide all the way to the right to complete the lesson.

exercises.title: Code lab: maxSumSubarray
exercises.content:
# Code lab: max sum of a window of size k

Implement `maxSumSubarray(nums, k)` returning the **largest sum of any contiguous subarray of length k**.

Do it in ONE pass: add the entering element, subtract the leaving one. No inner loop.

### Examples
- `maxSumSubarray([1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], 3)` → `16` (3+6+7)
- `maxSumSubarray([2,1,5,1,3,2], 3)` → `9` (5+1+3)
- `maxSumSubarray([5], 1)` → `5`
exercises.hint: The window is full for the first time at i = k - 1. From then on, every step is: +entering, -leaving, compare.

exercises.title: Pattern: Binary Search
exercises.content:
# Binary Search

On **sorted** data, one comparison against the middle element eliminates half the search space. n → n/2 → n/4 → … → 1 takes only **log₂(n)** steps: a million elements need just 20 comparisons.

### How to recognize it
- Sorted array, or a **monotonic** yes/no condition ("first version that fails", "smallest capacity that works")
- Interviewer hints at better than O(n)

### The template (memorize the boundaries!)
```javascript
let lo = 0, hi = nums.length - 1;
while (lo <= hi) {
  const mid = Math.floor((lo + hi) / 2);
  if (nums[mid] === target) return mid;
  if (nums[mid] < target) lo = mid + 1; // discard left half
  else hi = mid - 1;                    // discard right half
}
return -1;
```

### The three classic bugs
1. `while (lo < hi)` vs `lo <= hi` mismatch with the update rule
2. Forgetting `+1` / `-1` → infinite loop
3. Applying it to unsorted data

**Complexity:** O(log n) time, O(1) space.

**Classic problems:** Search Insert Position · First/Last Position · Search in Rotated Sorted Array · Koko Eating Bananas (search on the answer)

exercises.title: Animation: halving the space
exercises.content:
# Watch the search space halve

Find **25** in an 11-element sorted array.

Each step, predict: will `mid` be too big or too small, and which half gets discarded? Watch how fast the gray (discarded) region grows.

exercises.title: Code lab: binarySearch
exercises.content:
# Code lab: classic binary search

Implement `binarySearch(nums, target)` on a sorted array. Return the **index** of `target`, or `-1` if absent.

Get the boundaries right — this exact function is asked verbatim in phone screens.

### Examples
- `binarySearch([3,7,12,19,25,31,42,58,66,74,88], 25)` → `4`
- `binarySearch([1,3,5], 5)` → `2`
- `binarySearch([1,3,5], 2)` → `-1`
exercises.hint: Use lo <= hi with lo = mid + 1 and hi = mid - 1. If you use lo < hi you need a different update rule — don't mix the two.

exercises.title: Pattern: Fast & Slow Pointers
Animation: tortoise vs hare
exercises.content:
# Watch the hare catch the tortoise

A linked list `0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7` where node 7 loops back to node 3 (red dashed edge).

Advance both pointers and watch the gap close inside the cycle — the meeting is mathematically inevitable.

exercises.title: Quiz: cycle logic
exercises.content:
# Check your understanding

Three quick questions on why Floyd's algorithm works and where to reach for it.

quiz.prompt: Inside a cycle, how fast does the fast pointer close the gap on the slow pointer?
options: 2 nodes per step
1 node per step
It depends on the cycle length
The gap never closes
quiz.explanation: Slow moves +1 and fast moves +2, so the relative speed is exactly 1 node per step — the gap shrinks by 1 every step, guaranteeing a meeting.

quiz.prompt: The list has NO cycle. What happens with fast & slow pointers?
options: They meet at the last node
Infinite loop
fast (or fast.next) becomes null and we return false
slow overtakes fast
quiz.explanation: Without a cycle the fast pointer runs off the end — that's the termination condition `while (fast && fast.next)`.

quiz.prompt: What's the key advantage of fast & slow over storing visited nodes in a Set?
options: It's faster: O(log n) vs O(n)
It uses O(1) extra space instead of O(n)
It also works on unsorted arrays
It finds the longest cycle
quiz.explanation: Both are O(n) time. The win is space: two pointers instead of a Set holding up to n nodes.

exercises.title: Pattern: Monotonic Stack
Animation: pop then push
exercises.content:
# Watch the stack stay sorted

Process `[2, 1, 5, 3, 6, 2, 3]` while keeping the stack increasing bottom→top.

Before each click, predict: **which elements get popped?** Every pop means the current element is that value's next-greater answer.

exercises.title: Code lab: nextGreater
exercises.content:
# Code lab: next greater element

Implement `nextGreater(nums)` returning an array where position `i` holds the **first element to the right of `nums[i]` that is strictly greater**, or `-1` if none exists.

Use a monotonic stack of indexes — one pass, O(n).

### Examples
- `nextGreater([2,1,5,3,6,2,3])` → `[5,5,6,6,-1,3,-1]`
- `nextGreater([1,2,3])` → `[2,3,-1]`
- `nextGreater([3,2,1])` → `[-1,-1,-1]`
exercises.hint: Store indexes, not values — you need the index to write into `answer` when a pop happens. Whatever is left on the stack at the end keeps its -1.

exercises.title: Pattern: Merge Intervals
Animation: bars collapsing
exercises.content:
# Watch intervals collapse

Sweep through `[[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[9,12],[15,18]]` left to right.

At each step the question is binary: does the next bar **touch the current block** (extend it) or **leave a gap** (start a new one)? Five inputs collapse into three blocks.

exercises.title: Code lab: mergeIntervals
exercises.content:
# Code lab: merge overlapping intervals

Implement `mergeIntervals(intervals)` — merge all overlapping intervals and return the result sorted by start.

### Examples
- `mergeIntervals([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[9,12],[15,18]])` → `[[1,6],[8,12],[15,18]]`
- `mergeIntervals([[1,4],[4,5]])` → `[[1,5]]` (touching counts as overlap here)
- `mergeIntervals([[5,6],[1,2]])` → `[[1,2],[5,6]]` (don't forget to sort!)
exercises.hint: Sort first: intervals.sort((a, b) => a[0] - b[0]). Then compare each interval's start with the END of the last merged block.

exercises.title: Pattern: BFS
Animation: the expanding wave
exercises.content:
# Watch the wave expand

BFS from **S** to **E** through a maze. Each click expands the frontier by exactly one ring — the number in each cell is its distance from S.

Notice: the wave hits E exactly once, and that first touch **is** the shortest path.

exercises.title: Quiz: BFS vs DFS
exercises.content:
# Check your understanding

When the interviewer says "shortest", which traversal do you reach for — and why?

quiz.prompt: Why does BFS find the shortest path in an UNWEIGHTED graph?
options: It tries all paths and keeps the best
It visits nodes in order of distance, so the first arrival is via a shortest route
The queue sorts nodes by distance
It doesn't — you need Dijkstra
quiz.explanation: BFS processes all distance-k nodes before any distance-(k+1) node, so the first time it touches a node, no shorter route can exist. Dijkstra is only needed when edges have different weights.

quiz.prompt: When should a cell be marked as visited?
options: When it's popped off the queue
When it's pushed onto the queue
After the whole level is processed
Only if it's on the shortest path
quiz.explanation: Mark on push. If you mark on pop, the same cell can be queued many times by different neighbors, bloating the queue and breaking level counting.

quiz.prompt: "Count how many islands of 1s exist in a grid." Which approach works?
options: Only BFS
Only DFS
Either — you just need to visit every cell of each component
Neither — this needs dynamic programming
quiz.explanation: Counting connected components doesn't involve shortest distance, so BFS and DFS both work. Reach specifically for BFS when the question says SHORTEST or MINIMUM steps.

exercises.title: Pattern: Backtracking
exercises.content:
# Backtracking

Systematically explore a **decision tree**: make a choice, recurse deeper, then **UNDO the choice** and try the next option. Three moves, always the same: **choose → explore → un-choose**.

### How to recognize it
- "Generate **all** subsets / permutations / combinations…"
- "Place N queens…", "solve the Sudoku…" — constraint satisfaction
- The answer is a **set of solutions**, not a single number

### The template
```javascript
function backtrack(path, choices) {
  record(path);                 // or: if (isComplete(path)) record
  for (const choice of choices) {
    path.push(choice);          // choose
    backtrack(path, next(choices, choice)); // explore
    path.pop();                 // un-choose ← the "backtrack"
  }
}
```

### The two rules
1. **Undo exactly what you did** — push/pop must mirror perfectly.
2. **Copy the path when recording** (`[...path]`) — otherwise every recorded answer mutates later.

**Complexity:** output-bound — subsets are O(2ⁿ), permutations O(n!). Pruning (skip branches that can't work) is where interviews get interesting.

**Classic problems:** Subsets · Permutations · Combination Sum · N-Queens · Word Search · Sudoku Solver

exercises.title: Animation: explore & backtrack
exercises.content:
# Watch the search explore and retreat

A decision tree with three branches. Green = exploring deeper, blue dashed = backtracking after a dead end, orange = solution found.

Step through the full search and watch how every dead end triggers the same move: undo, go back up, try the next branch.

exercises.title: Code lab: subsets
exercises.content:
# Code lab: generate all subsets

Implement `subsets(nums)` returning **every subset** of `nums`, in the standard DFS order: record the current path at every node, then extend with each remaining element (by index, left to right).

For `[1,2,3]` that order is:

```
[] → [1] → [1,2] → [1,2,3] → [1,3] → [2] → [2,3] → [3]
```

### Examples
- `subsets([1,2,3])` → `[[],[1],[1,2],[1,2,3],[1,3],[2],[2,3],[3]]`
- `subsets([7])` → `[[],[7]]`
exercises.hint: Inside the loop: path.push(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1); path.pop(); — the pop IS the backtrack from the animation.

exercises.title: Pattern: Dynamic Programming
exercises.content:
# Dynamic Programming

DP is just **filling a table**: break the problem into subproblems, solve each once, reuse the answers. No magic — three moves every time:

1. **Define the state** — what does `dp[i]` (or `dp[i][j]`) mean in plain English?
2. **Find the transition** — how does a state combine earlier states? (`dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]`)
3. **Seed base cases and fill in dependency order.**

### How to recognize it
- "**How many ways**…", "**minimum/maximum** cost to reach…"
- Choices at each step + **overlapping subproblems** (naive recursion recomputes the same calls)
- Yes/no: "can you make amount X from these coins?"

### Example: Climbing Stairs
You climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Ways to reach step n?
```javascript
// dp[i] = ways to reach step i
// You arrived from i-1 (one step) or i-2 (two steps):
// dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]; dp[1] = 1, dp[2] = 2
```

### Top-down vs bottom-up
Memoized recursion (top-down) and table filling (bottom-up) are the same algorithm; interviews usually want you to at least sketch bottom-up.

**Classic problems:** Climbing Stairs · House Robber · Coin Change · Unique Paths · Longest Common Subsequence · Edit Distance

exercises.title: Animation: fill the table
exercises.content:
# Watch the DP table fill

**Unique Paths**: a robot walks from the top-left of a 4×4 grid to the bottom-right, moving only → and ↓.

Every cell's value is just *(ways from above)* + *(ways from the left)*. Fill the table and watch 20 paths get counted without exploring a single one.

exercises.title: Code lab: climbStairs
exercises.content:
# Code lab: climbing stairs

Implement `climbStairs(n)` — you can climb **1 or 2 steps** at a time; return how many distinct ways there are to reach step `n`.

State: `dp[i]` = ways to reach step `i`. Transition: `dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]`.

### Examples
- `climbStairs(2)` → `2` (1+1, 2)
- `climbStairs(5)` → `8`
- `climbStairs(10)` → `89`
exercises.hint: It's Fibonacci with different seeds: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13… Keep prev1/prev2 variables and roll them forward.

exercises.title: Pattern: DFS & tree traversal
exercises.content:
# DFS & Tree Traversal

**Depth-first search (DFS)** follows one path as far as it can before returning to try a sibling. In a tree, recursion gives you the stack for free: visit a node, recursively solve its children, then return.

### How to recognize it
- The input is a **tree, graph, grid, or nested structure**
- You need to explore a complete branch, find a path, count components, or validate a subtree
- The question asks for **preorder, inorder, postorder**, root-to-leaf paths, or connected components

### Preorder template
```javascript
function preorder(root, result = []) {
  if (!root) return result;
  result.push(root.value);       // visit node
  preorder(root.left, result);   // finish left branch
  preorder(root.right, result);  // then right branch
  return result;
}
```

### Traversal order matters
- **Preorder:** node -> left -> right. Useful for copying or serializing a tree.
- **Inorder:** left -> node -> right. On a BST, this produces sorted values.
- **Postorder:** left -> right -> node. Useful when children must be processed before a parent.

### The invariant
At any moment, the recursive call stack is exactly the path from the root to the node being explored. Mark a node as visited before recursing in a graph, otherwise cycles can recurse forever.

**Complexity:** O(V + E) for a graph, O(n) for a tree. Recursion or an explicit stack uses O(h) tree space, up to O(V) in the worst case.

**Classic problems:** Maximum Depth of Binary Tree · Path Sum · Validate BST · Number of Islands · Clone Graph · Course Schedule (with cycle detection)

exercises.title: Animation: follow one branch
exercises.content:
# Watch preorder DFS

Traverse the tree starting at **A** with preorder DFS: visit the node, complete its left subtree, then complete its right subtree.

Watch the **call stack** alongside the **preorder output**. Before each click, predict whether DFS goes deeper or returns to a sibling branch.

exercises.title: Code lab: preorderValues
exercises.content:
# Code lab: preorder traversal

Implement `preorderValues(root)` for a binary tree node shaped like `{ value, left, right }`. Return the values in **preorder**: node, then left subtree, then right subtree.

Use recursion and return an empty array for an empty tree.

### Example

```text
      1
     / \
    2   3
   / \   \
  4   5   6
```

`preorderValues(root)` -> `[1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6]`
exercises.hint: The first line inside dfs after the null guard is `values.push(node.value)`. Then call dfs on left and right in that order.

exercises.title: Pattern: divide and conquer
exercises.content:
# Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort

Split a problem into smaller independent problems, solve them, then combine the answers. Merge sort is the canonical example: split until one value remains, then merge sorted runs.

### Recognize it
- A problem can be divided into similar smaller subproblems
- Combining solved halves is cheaper than brute force
- You need predictable O(n log n) sorting or inversion counting

### Invariant
Every recursive call returns a sorted array. The merge step only compares the front of two already-sorted runs.

**Complexity:** O(n log n) time, O(n) extra space.

**Classic problems:** Sort an Array · Count Inversions · Merge K Sorted Lists · Reverse Pairs

exercises.title: Animation: split and merge
exercises.content:
# Watch merge sort

Split `[8,3,5,1]` into runs of one, then merge them back in sorted order. Each merge compares only the two run fronts.

exercises.title: Code lab: mergeSort
exercises.content:
# Code lab: merge sort

Implement `mergeSort(nums)` and return a new sorted array. Split recursively and merge two sorted halves.
exercises.hint: Return a new array from merge. Compare the front values of the two sorted halves.

exercises.title: Pattern: select without sorting
exercises.content:
# Quickselect

Quickselect partitions around a pivot like quicksort, but only recurses into the side containing the target rank.

### Recognize it
- You need the kth smallest/largest element
- Sorting every element is unnecessary
- A partition can tell you where the answer lives

**Complexity:** expected O(n), worst-case O(n²).

**Classic problems:** Kth Largest Element · Top K Frequent Elements · Median of an Unsorted Array

exercises.title: Animation: partition for k
exercises.content:
# Watch quickselect

Find the third-smallest value without sorting every candidate. The pivot's rank decides which partition survives.

exercises.title: Code lab: kthSmallest
exercises.content:
# Code lab: kth smallest

Implement `kthSmallest(nums, k)` where `k` is 1-indexed. Return the kth smallest value.
exercises.hint: After partitioning, compare the pivot index with k - 1.

exercises.title: Pattern: ordered events
exercises.content:
# Sweep Line

Turn starts, ends, points, or boundaries into ordered events. Sweep left to right while maintaining exactly the active state needed for the answer.

### Recognize it
- Intervals overlap on a timeline or coordinate axis
- You need maximum concurrency, total coverage, or collision counts
- Sorting event boundaries makes the active state simple

**Complexity:** typically O(n log n) for sorting plus O(n) for the sweep.

**Classic problems:** Meeting Rooms II · Car Pooling · Skyline Problem · Rectangle Overlap

exercises.title: Animation: sweep events
exercises.content:
# Watch a sweep line

Process meeting start and end events in time order. The active count reveals how many rooms are needed.

exercises.title: Code lab: maxConcurrent
exercises.content:
# Code lab: maximum concurrent intervals

Implement `maxConcurrent(intervals)` for half-open intervals `[start, end]`. Return the peak number of simultaneous intervals.
exercises.hint: Use +1 for a start and -1 for an end. Sort equal times with -1 first.

exercises.title: Pattern: deque of candidates
exercises.content:
# Monotonic Queue

A deque keeps candidates in decreasing or increasing order. When a stronger new value arrives, weaker tail values can never become the answer and are removed.

### Recognize it
- You need a maximum/minimum for every sliding window
- A normal queue cannot remove dominated candidates
- The window moves one position at a time

**Complexity:** O(n): every index enters and leaves the deque once.

**Classic problems:** Sliding Window Maximum · Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K · Constrained Subsequence Sum

exercises.title: Animation: keep only maxima
exercises.content:
# Watch a monotonic deque

Slide a size-3 window while discarding older, smaller values that can never become a future maximum.

exercises.title: Code lab: slidingWindowMax
exercises.content:
# Code lab: sliding window maximum

Implement `slidingWindowMax(nums, k)` using a deque of indexes.
exercises.hint: Remove expired indexes from the front and smaller values from the back.

exercises.title: Pattern: reuse prefix knowledge
exercises.content:
# String Matching: KMP & Rabin-Karp

KMP builds a longest-prefix-suffix table so a mismatch reuses known work. Rabin-Karp compares rolling hashes and verifies collisions when necessary.

### Recognize it
- Find a pattern inside a long text
- Many repeated prefix comparisons cause quadratic brute force
- You need repeated or multi-pattern matching

**Complexity:** KMP is O(n+m). Rabin-Karp is expected O(n+m) for one pattern.

**Classic problems:** Implement strStr · Repeated Substring Pattern · Shortest Palindrome

exercises.title: Animation: reuse a prefix
exercises.content:
# Watch KMP recover from a mismatch

Use a prefix table to continue after a mismatch instead of restarting the text scan.

exercises.title: Code lab: kmpIndexOf
exercises.content:
# Code lab: KMP search

Implement `kmpIndexOf(text, pattern)` and return the first match index or `-1`.
exercises.hint: On mismatch with j > 0, set j = lps[j - 1] rather than advancing text.

exercises.title: Pattern: dynamic components
exercises.content:
# Disjoint Set Union

Union Find maintains groups that merge over time. `find` returns a component root; `union` joins roots. Path compression and union by size make repeated queries extremely fast.

### Recognize it
- Edges arrive and you need connectivity after each merge
- You need to detect a cycle while building a graph
- The question asks for connected components or redundant connections

**Complexity:** O(alpha(n)) amortized per operation, effectively constant.

**Classic problems:** Number of Provinces · Redundant Connection · Accounts Merge · Kruskal MST

exercises.title: Animation: merge components
exercises.content:
# Watch Union Find

Merge edges one at a time, then watch path compression shorten future root lookups.

exercises.title: Code lab: countComponents
exercises.content:
# Code lab: count components

Implement `countComponents(n, edges)` for nodes `0..n-1` using Union Find.
exercises.hint: If two nodes already have the same root, do not decrement the component count.

exercises.title: Pattern: range queries with updates
exercises.content:
# Segment Tree & Fenwick Tree

These structures precompute partial aggregates so range sums, minimums, or counts can be queried and updated faster than scanning.

### Recognize it
- Many range queries plus updates
- Prefix sums alone are too slow after mutations
- You need sum/min/max/count over intervals

**Complexity:** O(log n) query and update. Fenwick trees are compact for invertible prefix aggregates; segment trees support richer combines.

**Classic problems:** Range Sum Query Mutable · Count Smaller After Self · My Calendar III

exercises.title: Animation: combine range aggregates
exercises.content:
# Watch a range query

Query an interval by combining only the tree nodes that exactly cover it, leaving unrelated values untouched.

exercises.title: Code lab: rangeSum
exercises.content:
# Code lab: Fenwick prefix sums

Implement `rangeSum(nums, left, right)` using a Fenwick tree built inside the function. Return the inclusive range sum.
exercises.hint: A Fenwick tree is 1-indexed. Update with i += i & -i and query with i -= i & -i.

exercises.title: Pattern: connect everything cheaply
exercises.content:
# Minimum Spanning Tree

An MST connects every vertex with minimum total edge weight and no cycles. Kruskal sorts edges and uses Union Find; Prim grows one connected frontier with a priority queue.

### Recognize it
- Connect all locations with minimum total cost
- Cycles are wasteful
- You are choosing edges, not one source-to-destination path

**Complexity:** Kruskal O(E log E); Prim O(E log V) with a heap.

**Classic problems:** Min Cost to Connect All Points · Connecting Cities With Minimum Cost · Optimize Water Distribution

exercises.title: Animation: accept safe edges
exercises.content:
# Watch Kruskal's algorithm

Consider edges from lightest to heaviest. Add only an edge that joins two separate components.

exercises.title: Code lab: kruskalWeight
exercises.content:
# Code lab: Kruskal total weight

Implement `kruskalWeight(n, edges)` where each edge is `[u, v, weight]`. Return the MST weight.
exercises.hint: Only add an edge's weight when its endpoints have different roots.

exercises.title: Pattern: repeated relaxation
exercises.content:
# Bellman-Ford & Floyd-Warshall

Relaxation asks whether an edge gives a cheaper route to its destination. Bellman-Ford repeats this from one source and supports negative edges; Floyd-Warshall tries every vertex as an intermediate for all-pairs distances.

### Recognize it
- Edge weights can be negative
- You need all-pairs distances or negative-cycle detection
- Dijkstra's nonnegative-weight assumption does not hold

**Complexity:** Bellman-Ford O(VE); Floyd-Warshall O(V³).

**Classic problems:** Cheapest Flights with K Stops · Network Delay variants · Arbitrage detection

exercises.title: Animation: relax weighted edges
exercises.content:
# Watch Bellman-Ford relaxations

Update a distance only when an edge proves a cheaper path. Negative edges are safe; a negative cycle is detected by one extra pass.

exercises.title: Code lab: bellmanFord
exercises.content:
# Code lab: Bellman-Ford distances

Implement `bellmanFord(n, edges, source)`. Edges are `[from, to, weight]`; return the distance array.
exercises.hint: Represent unreachable distances as null before returning so tests stay JSON-safe.

exercises.title: Pattern: residual capacity
exercises.content:
# Network Flow

Max flow repeatedly finds an augmenting path from source to sink, sends the bottleneck capacity, and updates residual edges. Reverse residual capacity lets later paths reroute earlier flow.

### Recognize it
- Allocate limited capacity through a network
- Match workers to jobs, route packets, or maximize throughput
- The question has a source, sink, capacities, and conservation constraints

**Complexity:** Edmonds-Karp runs in O(VE²).

**Classic problems:** Maximum Bipartite Matching · Course Scheduling with Capacities · Minimum Cut

exercises.title: Animation: augment residual paths
exercises.content:
# Watch max flow

Find a source-to-sink residual path, push its bottleneck flow, then repeat until no path remains.

exercises.title: Code lab: maxFlow
exercises.content:
# Code lab: Edmonds-Karp max flow

Implement `maxFlow(capacity, source, sink)` for an adjacency matrix using BFS over the residual graph.
exercises.hint: BFS records parents. The path flow is the minimum residual edge on that parent chain.
